metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or add) a fsck detection path for the all-reverses-failed sub-case: when a snapshot is present but ALL its source UUIDs resolve only to .retired files (none active), fsck should emit a distinct finding (e.g. INCOMPLETE_ROLLBACK) so that --repair-snapshots triggers the rebuild path even when zero SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT findings exist.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 asserts 'fsck deterministically detects and repairs the incomplete-rollback state, including the inner reverse-rename-failure sub-case (snapshot retained),' but the existing fsck code only triggers the repair path when SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT or ORPHAN_EVENT is found. In the degenerate all-reverses-failed sub-case (zero source files restored to .json, all remaining as .retired), no active .json source files exist to trigger SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT, and the retained snapshot's source UUIDs all point at .retired files that _check_snapshot excludes (the .json extension filter at fsck.py line 279 means .json.retired is already invisible). The code therefore emits no finding and fsck exits 0 — leaving the mixed state silently undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py lines 278–304
findings.suggested_fix: In `_check_snapshot` (or a new dedicated check in `_scan`), after computing `event_files` (active-only), count how many `source_uuids` appear in neither `event_files` (active) nor any `.retired` file. If all are in `.retired` and the snapshot is present, emit an `INCOMPLETE_ROLLBACK` or similar tag. Alternatively, extend the `_repair_plan` heuristic to detect a snapshot-present + all-sources-retired state and classify it as needing a rebuild.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to ONE repair approach (retain-snapshot OR recovery-sentinel) in the plan and add a brief positive rationale (e.g. 'we retain the snapshot because it already carries the folded state and the existing SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT fsck path covers the residual .retired files without new detection logic'); remove the 'or' branch.
findings.finding: The plan presents two structurally distinct mechanisms ('retain the snapshot OR write a recovery sentinel') in the What section and AC2 without selecting one, and provides no positive rationale for whichever approach is adopted. These differ materially: retaining the existing snapshot requires no new file and leverages the existing SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT fsck check, while a recovery sentinel requires a new file format, new fsck detection logic, and new repair path. Without a committed choice and rationale, the implementer faces an open design decision at coding time, and the AC2 fsck coverage requirement changes depending on which branch is taken.
findings.location: What section ('retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)') and AC2 ('The chosen policy for the residual mixed state … is documented')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)' with 'retain it — the snapshot already carries the full folded state, so no event data is lost; the existing fsck SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT detection covers the case where un-reversed sources remain as active .json files (if any), and .retired files that failed to un-retire are already invisible to normal replay and covered by the snapshot.' Update AC2 to remove the open policy question and confirm that no new fsck artifact class is introduced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fsck detection rule for the incomplete-rollback sub-case: name the new issue kind (e.g. `INCOMPLETE_ROLLBACK`), define what file-system evidence triggers it (snapshot present + at least one .retired file whose UUID is in source_event_uuids but whose un-retired counterpart is absent), and add it to `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` and `_repair_plan`.
findings.finding: AC3 requires fsck to 'deterministically detect and repair the incomplete-rollback state, including the inner reverse-rename-failure sub-case (snapshot retained),' but the plan does not specify what new detection rule or issue kind fsck uses for this state. The existing --repair-snapshots path triggers only on SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT, neither of which fires when stranded .retired files are excluded from the active-event scan by is_active_event — so the sub-case is silently undetected by the current fsck logic.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py lines 191-205 (_scan Check 4), lines 278-321 (_check_snapshot)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new fsck Check 4b: for each ticket, if a SNAPSHOT exists and any source UUID maps to a `.retired` file that has no active `.json` counterpart AND the non-retired path also does not exist as an active file, emit `INCOMPLETE_ROLLBACK: {ticket_id} — {n} source(s) stranded as *.retired after failed rollback`. Hook `_repair_plan` to handle `INCOMPLETE_ROLLBACK` via `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2 with a concrete assertion: after the fault injection, reading compacted state via the retained snapshot must equal pre-compaction state (e.g., deserialize snapshot + remaining active files and assert equality to the pre-test baseline).
findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'no source data lost' while simultaneously permitting 'some *.retired sources not restored', but provides no concrete check that the retained snapshot actually covers those unreachable *.retired files — the assertion is stated as policy, not as a measurable test outcome.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 (Reverse-rename fault test)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (or the AC2 assertion) the exact stderr/stdout string that 'recoverable diagnostic' means (e.g. `rollback incomplete; retained snapshot at <path>; run fsck --repair-snapshots`) so the fault-injection test can assert on a concrete, unambiguous message.
findings.finding: The plan asserts (in the 'What' section) 'emit an explicit `rollback incomplete; run fsck` diagnostic' as a new behaviour to add in compact.py, but the current rollback path already logs a warning via `logger.warning` (compact.py line 238–241) and writes `sys.stderr.write('Error: failed to retire folded events while holding lock\n')` (line 246). The plan does not clarify whether the proposed new diagnostic is the stderr line, a new structured sentinel file, or an additional signal — leaving it ambiguous whether the current stderr output satisfies the AC or new work is genuinely required.
findings.location: What section — 'emit an explicit rollback incomplete; run fsck diagnostic'; src/rebar/_commands/compact.py lines 235–247
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'assert stderr contains the literal string "rollback incomplete" and the snapshot path' (or equivalent) so the test is deterministic and the implementer knows what to write.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py` with `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` in the Scope list for the fsck repair path, since all repair/detection logic (`_repair_plan`, `_repair_ticket`, `_scan`) lives there.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py` is named as the edit target for 'fsck repair path (mixed-state recognition)', but this file contains only cache read/write/hash logic with no repair or detection code. All fsck repair logic (`_repair_plan`, `_repair_ticket`, `_scan`, `_check_snapshot`) lives in `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py`. The incomplete-rollback mixed-state detection and repair must be added to `fsck.py`, not `_cache.py`.
findings.location: Scope: `src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py` / fsck repair path (mixed-state recognition)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope line to: `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` (mixed-state detection in `_repair_plan` / `_check_snapshot`; retain `src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py` only if adding a new exported constant like `INCOMPLETE_ROLLBACK_SENTINEL`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC2, name the specific component(s) responsible for the 'snapshot-present skip tolerates residual *.retired' rule and confirm whether the existing compact.py idempotent-skip (line 222-223) is sufficient or whether the reducer's positional-skip path in _cache.py also requires an explicit guard.
findings.finding: AC2 states 'the snapshot-present skip must tolerate residual *.retired files so no committed state regresses' as a required policy, but does not specify which component owns this rule, whether it already holds (compact.py line 222-223 already skips existing .retired targets), or whether changes to _cache.py's prepare_event_files or the reducer are needed — leaving the processing rule unanchored.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; src/rebar/_commands/compact.py lines 222-223; src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py prepare_event_files

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC3 to specify the concrete post-repair end-state: e.g., 'after `fsck --repair-snapshots`: exit 0, all *.retired files renamed to their original names, snapshot file absent (consumed), and cache re-scan returns the full pre-compaction history'.
findings.finding: AC3 states fsck 'deterministically detects and repairs the incomplete-rollback state' but does not define the measurable post-repair end-state (e.g., expected exit code, which files exist/are-renamed, final cache visibility), making the criterion unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 (fsck criterion)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an 'idempotent re-compaction after fsck --repair-snapshots' assertion to AC2, mirroring AC1's structure, to verify that re-compaction of the mixed state (retained snapshot + *.retired files) completes cleanly and produces a result deep-equal to the pre-compaction state.
findings.finding: AC2 (reverse-rename fault test) does not require verifying idempotent re-compaction after the mixed state (snapshot retained + some *.retired files not restored), even though the 'Why' section's core concern is that subsequent replay or re-compaction is broken or produces data loss. AC1 explicitly requires 'an idempotent re-compaction' assertion for the clean-rollback case, creating an asymmetry: the hardest failure mode — which the plan identifies as the true hazard — has weaker post-condition coverage than the simpler case.
findings.location: AC2 (Reverse-rename fault test) vs. 'Why' section and AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'After fsck --repair-snapshots, a subsequent compaction run completes without error and yields reduced state deep-equal to the pre-compaction state (idempotent re-compaction).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC assertions for the post-fsck-repair state: enumerate the exact expected file-system layout (no *.retired files, snapshot removed or promoted, all active source filenames restored) and require that re-running fsck --repair-snapshots on an already-repaired repository is idempotent (exits 0, no state change).
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria do not assert the exact on-disk file-system state AFTER fsck --repair-snapshots completes (post-repair schema state), only that fsck 'detects and repairs' — a repair that exits 0 while leaving *.retired files or a stale snapshot is not caught by the stated ACs.
findings.location: AC: 'fsck deterministically detects and repairs the incomplete-rollback state, including the inner reverse-rename-failure sub-case (snapshot retained)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the fsck AC to read: 'After fsck --repair-snapshots: (1) no *.retired files remain, (2) the retained snapshot is either promoted to active or removed depending on whether all sources were restored, (3) active sources are byte-for-byte identical to pre-compaction state, and (4) a second invocation of fsck --repair-snapshots is a no-op (idempotent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC3 to specify exactly which fsck finding covers the incomplete-rollback state: if the existing SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT check (restored .json sources alongside the retained snapshot) is sufficient, say so; if the plan requires a new fsck check for the retained-snapshot + all-.retired sub-case, define the new finding kind and its repair action.
findings.finding: The AC2 and AC3 fsck requirements are ambiguous about what the 'residual mixed state' actually looks like after a reverse-rename failure, and whether the EXISTING fsck checks cover it without new code. In the incomplete-rollback scenario: some sources were forward-renamed to .retired (succeeded), the rollback reversed some of those back to .json (succeeded), but failed to reverse others (which remain .retired). The retained snapshot covers all of them. Files that remain .retired are NOT SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT (they're correctly retired); files restored to .json ARE SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT. The existing _check_snapshot/_repair_plan already handles SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT. But the plan's AC3 says fsck 'deterministically detects and repairs the incomplete-rollback state, including the inner reverse-rename-failure sub-case' — it is unclear if this means new detection is required or the existing SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT check suffices.
findings.location: AC3: 'fsck deterministically detects and repairs the incomplete-rollback state, including the inner reverse-rename-failure sub-case (snapshot retained)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'retain OR write a recovery sentinel' fork in the 'What' section: pick one approach, document it explicitly, and update AC3 to name the specific artifact (retained snapshot + co-present *.retired files, or a named sentinel file) that fsck uses to detect the incomplete-rollback state.
findings.finding: The 'What' section presents two alternative implementation strategies ('retain it OR write a recovery sentinel') without resolving which is chosen, but AC3 requires fsck to detect and repair the resulting state without specifying what artifact it looks for. These are structurally different: a retained snapshot looks identical to a successful forward-pass snapshot, whereas a sentinel is a distinct marker. The fsck detection logic differs fundamentally between the two options, yet no AC or scope entry documents which approach is adopted.
findings.location: 'What' section (first bullet) and AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Choose one: (a) 'retain snapshot + presence of *.retired files = incomplete rollback' (requires fsck to check for *.retired files alongside a snapshot), or (b) 'write a .rollback-incomplete sentinel alongside the snapshot' (simpler fsck detection). Document the chosen artifact in both the What section and AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the recovery sentinel: specify its filename convention (e.g. `.compact-incomplete`), its content schema (at minimum: ticket_id, timestamp, list of un-restored source paths), and the corresponding new fsck check that detects and reports it.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'recovery sentinel' as an alternative artifact ('retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)') but never defines its name, content schema, or the fsck detection rule that would recognise it — leaving the sentinel as an undefined schema/artifact whose vocabulary an implementer cannot derive from the plan alone.
findings.location: What section — 'retain it (or write a recovery sentinel) and emit an explicit `rollback incomplete; run fsck` diagnostic'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and test) the behavior of a re-compaction attempt on the mixed residual state (snapshot retained + some *.retired): either document it as safe/idempotent (and add it to AC2's assertions) or specify that the lock/fsck-sentinel blocks re-compaction until `fsck --repair-snapshots` clears the state.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether re-compaction (retry) on the mixed residual state — snapshot retained + some *.retired sources not restored — is safe or blocked until fsck runs. AC1 requires idempotent re-compaction for the forward-rename-fault case but AC2's mixed state is not given the same treatment, leaving the concurrency/idempotency contract undefined for the most dangerous residual state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (Reverse-rename fault test) and 'What' section policy statement
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'and an attempted re-compaction on the mixed state either (a) is blocked with a clear error directing the user to run fsck, or (b) is proven idempotent (i.e., it detects the retained snapshot, skips re-folding already-retired sources, and produces the same result as a clean compaction).' Choose one policy and test it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and test the non-happy-path of `fsck --repair-snapshots` for the incomplete-rollback mixed state: specify the exit code, user-visible message, and resulting filesystem state when fsck's own repair rename fails, so the user is never left without actionable feedback.
findings.finding: The UX non-happy-path for `fsck --repair-snapshots` itself is not specified: the plan names fsck as the recovery action but does not define what the user sees or what happens if fsck fails during repair of the mixed state (e.g., another OSError while fsck tries to rename *.retired back to active).
findings.location: AC3 ('fsck deterministically detects and repairs') and 'What' section ('emit an explicit rollback incomplete; run fsck diagnostic')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 or a new AC: 'If fsck --repair-snapshots encounters an error during repair, it exits nonzero with a message identifying the unrecoverable file(s) and instructs the user to manually resolve; the pre-repair state is preserved (no partial fsck changes committed).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` to the Scope list (or explicitly confirm it needs no changes) since it exercises the `fsck --repair-snapshots` path that AC3 extends.
findings.finding: `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` (line 539) explicitly exercises `fsck --repair-snapshots` against the live repair path and is not enumerated in Scope. Changes to what `_repair_plan` detects and `_repair_ticket` repairs for the new incomplete-rollback mixed state (snapshot retained + some `*.retired` not restored) may require this integration test to be extended or updated.
findings.location: Scope section (unenumerated consumer)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement (in Scope or AC) that fsck --repair-snapshots is idempotent and resumable: each individual rename-back operation must be conditional on the current file-system state (i.e., skip if target already exists and source *.retired does not), so a partially-interrupted repair run can be safely re-executed.
findings.finding: The plan does not address resumability of the fsck --repair-snapshots operation itself: if fsck is interrupted mid-repair (e.g., process killed while promoting the snapshot), re-running it may encounter a partially-repaired mixed state that it was not designed to handle, potentially double-applying rename operations.
findings.location: AC: 'fsck deterministically detects and repairs the incomplete-rollback state' / Scope: 'src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py / fsck repair path'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a single rollback-failure strategy in the What section — either 'retain the snapshot in place' or 'write a named recovery sentinel file' — and remove the ambiguous 'or'; update AC2/AC3 to name the chosen artifact.
findings.finding: The What section presents two mutually exclusive implementation strategies ('retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)') without choosing one, deferring a real design decision to the executor that affects the fsck repair logic and AC2/AC3.
findings.location: What section, bullet 1: 'do not delete the snapshot; retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)' with 'retain the snapshot in place (do not delete it)' since AC2 already says 'the snapshot is NOT deleted' — the sentinel branch contradicts the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC1's 'reduced state deep-equal to pre-compaction' (e.g., 'serialize reducer state to JSON before and after; assert JSON equality') and define 'idempotent re-compaction' (e.g., 'run compact a second time; assert exit 0, no new *.retired files, state JSON unchanged').
findings.finding: AC1 asserts 'reduced state deep-equal to pre-compaction' and 'idempotent re-compaction' but provides no concrete proving command or measurement mechanism for either claim.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 (Forward-rename fault test)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or design-doc section that formally specifies the cross-component contract: the exact residual state compact leaves on incomplete rollback (snapshot retained XOR sentinel written, with sentinel format), and the fsck detection/repair protocol that depends on it.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-component contract between compact.py (retain-snapshot/recovery-sentinel on rollback failure) and fsck (mixed-state detection/repair), but does not specify this interface formally, justify the coupling, or capture the architectural decision (retain-vs-sentinel policy, mixed-state definition) in an ADR or design doc.
findings.location: What section / Scope / AC: Reverse-rename fault test
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or a `docs/decisions/compaction-rollback-atomicity.md`) covering: (1) the chosen policy (retain snapshot, not sentinel, or vice versa — resolve the 'or'); (2) the exact mixed-state invariant fsck must detect (snapshot present AND *.retired files exist → incomplete rollback); (3) the repair steps fsck takes. Reference this ADR from both compact.py and the fsck repair path as a comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'retain snapshot OR write sentinel' ambiguity to a single policy and specify the error handling if that policy's write operation (sentinel write or snapshot fsync) fails — ensuring the fallback never silently drops recovery information.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)' without resolving which strategy is chosen and without specifying what happens if the sentinel write itself fails — introducing a new unhandled write-failure path that could recreate the original data-loss hazard at one level deeper.
findings.location: What section: 'On any reverse-rename failure during rollback, do not delete the snapshot; retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)'
findings.suggested_fix: Choose one policy (recommended: retain the snapshot unconditionally — it requires no additional write). If a sentinel is written, wrap it in a try/except that, on failure, logs a CRITICAL-level message and does NOT proceed to delete the snapshot, so the snapshot always remains as the recovery artifact when sentinel writing fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Caller Impact' section (or note in ## What) that explicitly audits every code path that branches on snapshot presence after a failed compaction (e.g., replay driver, health-check, monitoring) and documents that each either tolerates the retained-snapshot state or is updated accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan changes rollback behavior so that a retained snapshot can exist after a failed compaction, but it does not enumerate or acknowledge callers outside of `fsck` that may depend on the old invariant ('snapshot absent = compaction failed cleanly'). If any consumer (e.g., a CI health-check, a replay driver, or a monitoring probe) inspects snapshot presence to infer compaction state, it will misinterpret the retained snapshot as a completed compaction.
findings.location: ## What (rollback policy change) / AC – Reverse-rename fault test
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## What section, add a bullet: 'Audit callers of snapshot-presence post-compaction (replay path, fsck, any health/monitoring probes) and confirm each path correctly distinguishes "retained snapshot = incomplete rollback" from "snapshot = completed compaction"; update any that conflate the two.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Eliminate the 'or write a recovery sentinel' ambiguity: commit to retaining the snapshot as the sole recovery artifact (do not delete it on any rollback path), and add an AC sub-case covering the double-failure scenario (rename fails + snapshot already gone) so DATA LOSS RISK is explicitly acknowledged and handled.
findings.finding: The plan does not commit to a single rollback policy — it says 'retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)' — leaving open a path where only a sentinel is written. If the sentinel write itself fails (e.g., disk full after the snapshot is already deleted in a prior branch), no recovery artifact exists and data loss is possible with no detection path.
findings.location: What section: 'On any reverse-rename failure during rollback, do not delete the snapshot; retain it (or write a recovery sentinel)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the 'or write a recovery sentinel' alternative. State policy as: the snapshot MUST NOT be deleted if any reverse-rename fails; the sentinel, if used, is additive (written after snapshot retention is confirmed), never a replacement. Add AC language: 'If the snapshot cannot be retained (e.g., already deleted before failure detected), emit a CRITICAL diagnostic and leave *.retired files in place; fsck --repair-snapshots must detect this sub-state via *.retired presence without a matching snapshot.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
